subtopic: Type 2 diabetes diagnosis (ADS / RACGP)
stem: A 56-year-old man attends his GP for review of fatigue and nocturia over the last three months. He has hypertension treated with perindopril 5 mg daily and a BMI of 31 kg/m^2. He is asymptomatic of polyuria today and denies weight loss, vomiting or thirst beyond mild dry mouth. His father has type 2 diabetes. He has no acute illness and is not on glucocorticoids. The GP performs opportunistic screening. A fasting venous plasma glucose taken in a NATA-accredited laboratory two weeks ago was 7.3 mmol/L; today's HbA1c on a standardised laboratory assay is 50 mmol/mol (6.7 percent). A random venous glucose taken today is 9.4 mmol/L. He has no features of intercurrent illness, sepsis or recent steroid use that would invalidate the HbA1c. He has never had a previous diabetes diagnosis recorded.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/84 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, BMI 31 kg/m^2.
data_table.Examination: Acanthosis nigricans over the posterior neck. No ketotic breath. Hydrated.
data_table.Investigations: Fasting venous glucose 7.3 mmol/L (two weeks ago, NATA lab); HbA1c 50 mmol/mol (6.7 percent) today, standardised assay; random glucose 9.4 mmol/L today; eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m^2; ACR 1.2 mg/mmol.

lead_in: Which statement best describes how this patient meets the Australian diagnostic criteria for type 2 diabetes?

options.text: The single random venous glucose of 9.4 mmol/L on its own confirms diabetes
options.rationale: A random glucose only confirms diabetes if it is at least 11.1 mmol/L AND accompanied by classic symptoms; 9.4 mmol/L is below threshold (RACGP Management of T2D 2024; ADS Position Statement 2020).

options.text: Two separate abnormal results meet the criteria: fasting glucose 7.3 and HbA1c 50 mmol/mol
options.rationale: In an asymptomatic patient Australian diagnosis requires two abnormal tests, either two of the same or two different. Fasting venous glucose at least 7.0 mmol/L and HbA1c at least 48 mmol/mol (6.5 percent) on a standardised assay are each diagnostic when confirmed on a separate occasion (RACGP 2024; ADS 2020).

options.text: He does not meet criteria because no oral glucose tolerance test has been performed
options.rationale: OGTT is one of several diagnostic options but is not mandatory; HbA1c at least 48 mmol/mol or fasting glucose at least 7.0 mmol/L are equally accepted in asymptomatic adults (RACGP 2024).

options.text: He has impaired fasting glucose only and should simply be reviewed in 12 months
options.rationale: Impaired fasting glucose is 6.1 to 6.9 mmol/L; a fasting glucose of 7.0 mmol/L or greater meets the diabetes threshold and his HbA1c is above 48 mmol/mol (RACGP 2024).

options.text: HbA1c cannot be used here because acanthosis nigricans acts as a confounder
options.rationale: HbA1c is invalid in haemoglobinopathy, recent transfusion, haemolysis, pregnancy and rapidly evolving glycaemia; acanthosis nigricans is a clinical sign of insulin resistance and does not interfere with HbA1c (ADS 2020).

explanation.summary: In an asymptomatic adult, Australian guidance diagnoses type 2 diabetes by two abnormal tests on separate occasions: fasting venous glucose at least 7.0 mmol/L, HbA1c at least 48 mmol/mol (6.5 percent) on a standardised assay, OGTT 2-hour at least 11.1 mmol/L, or a single random glucose at least 11.1 mmol/L plus classic symptoms (RACGP 2024).
key_points: Fasting glucose >= 7.0 mmol/L is diagnostic when confirmed
HbA1c >= 48 mmol/mol (6.5 percent) on standardised assay is diagnostic when confirmed
Random glucose >= 11.1 mmol/L is diagnostic only with classic symptoms
Two abnormal results required in asymptomatic patients
explanation.pearls: Avoid HbA1c if haemoglobinopathy, recent blood loss, transfusion, pregnancy or rapidly changing glycaemia.

subtopic: T2DM escalation - cardiorenal indication for SGLT2 inhibitor
stem: A 64-year-old man with type 2 diabetes for 9 years attends his GP for diabetes review. He is on metformin XR 2 g daily and has been adherent for 18 months. He has heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (LVEF 35 percent) on echocardiography after an anterior STEMI two years ago, treated with stented LAD, and is on perindopril 8 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, atorvastatin 80 mg and aspirin 100 mg. He has chronic kidney disease with eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73m^2 and ACR 6.4 mg/mmol on two occasions. He has no symptoms of urinary tract infection, no recurrent thrush and no history of diabetic foot ulceration. BMI is 29 kg/m^2 and he is normotensive. His most recent HbA1c is 62 mmol/mol (7.8 percent), up from 56 mmol/mol six months ago. He denies hypoglycaemic episodes and is keen to optimise his cardiovascular and renal protection in addition to glycaemic control. He has no history of pancreatitis and no personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2. He has never had a diabetic foot ulcer, retinal laser or amputation. He is on no NSAID, no potassium-sparing diuretic and has no documented contrast allergy. He fully understands sick-day rules and is independently mobile, performs his own injections if needed and has reliable transport for monitoring bloods.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/72 mmHg, HR 64 bpm.
data_table.Examination: JVP not elevated. Chest clear. No peripheral oedema. Feet warm, pulses present, monofilament intact.
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 62 mmol/mol (7.8 percent); eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73m^2; ACR 6.4 mg/mmol; potassium 4.2 mmol/L; LFTs normal; LVEF 35 percent.
data_table.Medications: Metformin XR 2 g, perindopril 8 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, atorvastatin 80 mg, aspirin 100 mg.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in pharmacological management?

options.text: Add a sulfonylurea such as gliclazide MR 30 mg daily
options.rationale: Sulfonylureas lower HbA1c but carry hypoglycaemia risk, do not improve cardiorenal outcomes and offer no benefit in heart failure or CKD progression (RACGP 2024; ADS-ADEA Position Statement on T2DM).

options.text: Add an SGLT2 inhibitor such as empagliflozin 10 mg daily
options.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitors reduce cardiovascular death and heart failure hospitalisation in HFrEF and slow CKD progression with albuminuria; they are first-choice add-on for T2DM with established HFrEF and CKD with eGFR within approved range (ADS-ADEA Algorithm; Heart Foundation HFrEF guideline 2022; KHA-CARI / KDIGO 2022).

options.text: Add basal insulin glargine starting 10 units nocte
options.rationale: Basal insulin reduces HbA1c but is not the first-choice add-on when cardiorenal protection is the priority and the patient is not glucotoxic; insulin offers no cardiovascular outcome benefit (ADS-ADEA Algorithm).

options.text: Add a DPP4 inhibitor such as sitagliptin 100 mg daily
options.rationale: DPP4 inhibitors are weight neutral and well tolerated but do not improve cardiovascular or renal outcomes; saxagliptin specifically increases heart failure hospitalisation (ADS-ADEA Algorithm; AMH).

options.text: Add pioglitazone 15 mg daily for insulin sensitisation
options.rationale: Thiazolidinediones cause fluid retention and are contraindicated in heart failure; they are not appropriate in HFrEF (AMH; ADS-ADEA Algorithm).

explanation.summary: In T2DM with established HFrEF, CKD with albuminuria, or atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, an SGLT2 inhibitor is the preferred add-on regardless of HbA1c, because cardiovascular death, heart failure hospitalisation and CKD progression are reduced independently of glucose lowering (ADS-ADEA; Heart Foundation 2022; KDIGO 2022).
key_points: SGLT2i indicated in HFrEF irrespective of diabetes status
SGLT2i slows CKD progression when ACR is elevated and eGFR is above the threshold (typically >=20 mL/min/1.73m^2 for empagliflozin / dapagliflozin)
Counsel on euglycaemic DKA, sick-day rules, genital mycotic infection
GLP1 receptor agonist (semaglutide, dulaglutide) is the preferred add-on if ASCVD predominates and weight loss is needed
explanation.pearls: Hold SGLT2 inhibitor during intercurrent illness, fasting, or pre-operatively to reduce euglycaemic DKA risk.

subtopic: T2DM insulin initiation - basal regimen choice
stem: A 71-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 14 years attends her GP. She is on metformin XR 2 g, empagliflozin 25 mg and dulaglutide 1.5 mg weekly. She has tolerated triple therapy for 18 months without hypoglycaemia, weight loss has plateaued and her HbA1c has risen from 58 to 76 mmol/mol (9.1 percent) over the last six months. She has no acute illness, drinks no alcohol and lives independently with her husband who can assist with injections. She is willing to start insulin but anxious about night-time hypoglycaemia. Her eGFR is 62 mL/min/1.73m^2, weight 78 kg, and she has retinopathy on annual review. Fasting capillary glucose self-monitoring shows readings of 11 to 14 mmol/L; pre-dinner readings are 8 to 10 mmol/L. There is no significant post-prandial glucose excursion documented. She drives a private car only and her vision is corrected to 6/9. She is up to date with her sick-day plan for the SGLT2 inhibitor.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 134/78 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, weight 78 kg.
data_table.Examination: Unremarkable. No injection sites yet.
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 76 mmol/mol (9.1 percent); eGFR 62; fasting capillary glucose 11 to 14 mmol/L; pre-dinner 8 to 10 mmol/L.
data_table.Medications: Metformin XR 2 g, empagliflozin 25 mg, dulaglutide 1.5 mg weekly.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate insulin regimen to commence?

options.text: Premixed biphasic insulin (e.g. NovoMix 30) twice daily before breakfast and dinner
options.rationale: Premixed regimens cover both basal and prandial needs but carry the highest hypoglycaemia risk and least flexibility; they are not first line when only fasting glucose is elevated (RACGP 2024).

options.text: Basal-bolus regimen with insulin glargine nocte and rapid-acting insulin with each meal
options.rationale: Basal-bolus is reserved for failure of basal alone or significant prandial excursions; this patient has isolated fasting hyperglycaemia and no major post-prandial excursion (RACGP 2024).

options.text: Long-acting basal insulin glargine 10 units nocte titrated by 2 units every 3 days
options.rationale: Once-daily long-acting basal insulin is the preferred initiation strategy in T2DM with elevated fasting glucose; start 10 units (or 0.1 to 0.2 units/kg) nocte and titrate to fasting target, continuing metformin, SGLT2i and GLP1 RA (RACGP 2024; ADS-ADEA Algorithm).

options.text: Twice-daily NPH insulin in equal split doses administered each morning and again each night
options.rationale: NPH has a pronounced peak at 4 to 8 hours and higher nocturnal hypoglycaemia risk compared with long-acting analogues; long-acting analogues are preferred in older patients fearful of hypoglycaemia (RACGP 2024).

options.text: Prandial-only rapid-acting insulin aspart given with only the largest carbohydrate meal of the day
options.rationale: Prandial-only insulin addresses post-prandial excursions, not fasting hyperglycaemia, and would not correct her dominant abnormality (RACGP 2024).

explanation.summary: When non-insulin therapy fails in T2DM, the standard initiation is a once-daily long-acting basal insulin (glargine, degludec or detemir) nocte, with self-titration to a fasting target of 6 to 8 mmol/L. Existing metformin, SGLT2i and GLP1 RA should be continued (RACGP 2024; ADS-ADEA Algorithm).
key_points: Start 10 units nocte or 0.1 to 0.2 units/kg/day
Titrate by 2 units every 3 days based on fasting capillary glucose
Continue oral agents and GLP1 RA
Add prandial insulin only if basal target is met but HbA1c is still high
explanation.pearls: Always counsel on hypoglycaemia recognition, glucagon access, sick-day rules and Austroads driving guidance before starting insulin.

subtopic: DKA initial management - fluid before insulin
stem: A 22-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to ED with 18 hours of vomiting, polyuria and abdominal pain after running out of insulin glargine three days ago. She is alert and oriented but exhausted. Her usual basal-bolus regimen is glargine 18 units nocte and aspart with meals. She has no chest pain and no focal sepsis. On arrival she is tachycardic, hypotensive and breathing deeply. Capillary glucose is high (off the meter), capillary ketones are 5.8 mmol/L and a venous blood gas shows pH 7.10, bicarbonate 7 mmol/L, anion gap 28. Serum potassium on the gas is 5.4 mmol/L. Sodium is 132 mmol/L, creatinine 118 micromol/L, eGFR 52 mL/min/1.73m^2. She has voided 200 mL since arrival 30 minutes ago. ECG shows sinus tachycardia without peaked T waves. Urinalysis confirms ketonuria and glycosuria; there is no leucocyte esterase or nitrites and her CXR is clear.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 92/58 mmHg, HR 122 bpm, RR 28 (Kussmaul), T 36.8, SpO2 99 percent on RA, GCS 15.
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, capillary refill 3 seconds. Abdomen soft, generalised mild tenderness. No focal sepsis.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 'HI' (>33 mmol/L); ketones 5.8 mmol/L; pH 7.10, HCO3 7, anion gap 28; K+ 5.4, Na+ 132, Cr 118; ECG sinus tachycardia.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management within the first 15 minutes?

options.text: IV 0.9 percent sodium chloride 500 mL bolus over 15 to 30 minutes then reassess
options.rationale: Fluid resuscitation precedes insulin in DKA. Adult Australian and JBDS-IP guidance recommend an initial 500 to 1000 mL crystalloid bolus when systolic BP is below 90 mmHg, with reassessment before further boluses (eTG Endocrinology; JBDS DKA 2023).

options.text: Commence fixed-rate IV insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour without any IV fluid
options.rationale: Starting insulin before volume restoration worsens hypotension, accelerates potassium shift and can precipitate cardiovascular collapse; fluid is the priority (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Administer IV sodium bicarbonate 50 mmol as an immediate stat bolus dose
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is not recommended in DKA except in severe acidaemia with pH below 6.9 or life-threatening hyperkalaemia, because it worsens intracellular acidosis and risks cerebral oedema (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Give IV potassium chloride 40 mmol prior to any insulin or other therapy
options.rationale: Potassium replacement is withheld while serum potassium is above 5.5 mmol/L; her potassium is 5.4 mmol/L. Insulin will shift K+ intracellularly, so K+ replacement is added once K+ falls below 5.5 (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Subcutaneous long-acting insulin glargine 18 units administered immediately without IV fluid
options.rationale: Restarting basal insulin is recommended early but does not replace IV insulin infusion in established DKA and does not address the priority of volume resuscitation in shock (JBDS DKA 2023).

explanation.summary: DKA management begins with IV fluid resuscitation, then a fixed-rate IV insulin infusion (0.1 units/kg/hour), with potassium added once K+ falls below 5.5 mmol/L and dextrose added once glucose falls below 14 mmol/L. Bicarbonate is reserved for pH <6.9 (eTG Endocrinology; JBDS DKA 2023).
key_points: Volume resuscitation first when shocked (500 to 1000 mL crystalloid bolus)
Insulin infusion 0.1 units/kg/hour after volume started
K+ replacement when serum K+ <5.5 mmol/L; hold insulin if K+ <3.3 mmol/L
Continue long-acting basal insulin throughout to bridge transition off IV insulin
explanation.pearls: Resolution criteria: ketones <0.6 mmol/L AND venous pH >=7.30 AND HCO3 >=18 mmol/L.

subtopic: Paediatric DKA cerebral oedema - risk recognition in young adult
stem: An 18-year-old woman with newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes is admitted to a regional ED with severe DKA. Capillary glucose is 38 mmol/L, ketones 6.4 mmol/L, venous pH 6.98, HCO3 5 mmol/L, sodium 128 mmol/L, potassium 3.6 mmol/L, urea 11 mmol/L, creatinine 132 micromol/L. She received 2 L of 0.9 percent saline over the first hour for shock, followed by 0.9 percent saline at 500 mL/hour and a fixed-rate insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour. Five hours into treatment, blood glucose has fallen to 12 mmol/L and bicarbonate has risen to 11 mmol/L, but she develops a worsening frontal headache, increasing drowsiness with a GCS drop from 15 to 11, a single episode of vomiting and a slowing heart rate. Her blood pressure has risen from 102/64 to 142/86 mmHg. Capillary glucose is now 11 mmol/L and ketones 2.4 mmol/L. The receiving consultant is concerned about a deteriorating neurological picture. A bedside neurological exam reveals sluggish pupils and brisk lower limb reflexes. She has no haemoglobinopathy, no known intracranial pathology and no history of seizures. There was no head injury or fall before the deterioration. Retrieval to a tertiary paediatric ICU is being considered and the airway tray, mannitol, hypertonic saline and intubation drugs are at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 142/86 mmHg (up from 102/64), HR 58 bpm (down from 118), RR 18, SpO2 99 percent, T 36.6, GCS 11 (E3 V3 M5).
data_table.Examination: Drowsy. Sluggish pupillary response, no focal weakness. Brisk LL reflexes.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 11 mmol/L (was 38); ketones 2.4; pH 7.18, HCO3 11; Na 130, K 3.8.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Increase the IV fluid infusion rate to correct the remaining estimated deficit much faster
options.rationale: Excessive fluid administration is a contributor to cerebral oedema in DKA; the appropriate response to suspected cerebral oedema is to halve or pause maintenance fluids, not increase them (BSPED / RCH CPG DKA).

options.text: Hypertonic saline 5 mL/kg over 10 minutes, halve fluids, head up, CT and retrieval
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline (or mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg) is first-line for suspected cerebral oedema in DKA. Halving fluids, head-up positioning, securing airway and urgent imaging plus retrieval to a tertiary centre are concurrent priorities (RCH CPG: DKA; BSPED 2020).

options.text: Stop the IV insulin infusion entirely and simply observe her for any spontaneous improvement
options.rationale: Stopping insulin allows ketogenesis to resume and does not treat the rising intracranial pressure; insulin should be continued (often reduced) while neuroprotective measures are delivered (RCH CPG: DKA).

options.text: Give IV 50 percent dextrose 50 mL because the cause of drowsiness is hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Glucose 11 mmol/L is not hypoglycaemic; when DKA glucose falls below 14 mmol/L, the correct action is to add 5 or 10 percent dextrose to the maintenance fluid rather than push concentrated dextrose (RCH CPG: DKA).

options.text: Administer IV ceftriaxone 2 g and dexamethasone 10 mg for presumed bacterial meningitis
options.rationale: While headache and drowsiness raise meningitis as a differential, the pattern of Cushing reflex (hypertension, bradycardia) and timing in DKA make cerebral oedema the most likely diagnosis; empirical antibiotics should not delay osmotherapy (RCH CPG: DKA).

explanation.summary: Cerebral oedema is a feared complication of DKA, especially in younger patients and the first 4 to 12 hours of treatment. Risk factors include severe acidosis, low partial pressure of CO2, raised urea, bicarbonate administration and aggressive fluid replacement. Treatment is osmotherapy (hypertonic saline or mannitol), reduced fluid rate, head-up positioning and urgent imaging plus retrieval (RCH CPG; BSPED 2020).
key_points: Triad of headache, decreased GCS and Cushing reflex in DKA = cerebral oedema until proven otherwise
Hypertonic saline 3 percent 5 mL/kg OR mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg
Halve maintenance fluids, head up 30 degrees, secure airway if GCS drops further
Do NOT correct sodium too rapidly; corrected sodium should rise as glucose falls
explanation.pearls: Late teens with first presentation DKA, severe acidosis and hyponatraemia carry the highest risk - low threshold for retrieval to a paediatric ICU.

subtopic: HHS vs DKA - discrimination
stem: A 78-year-old man with type 2 diabetes is brought from his nursing home with one week of progressive confusion and reduced oral intake. He has hypertension, chronic kidney disease and a recent UTI treated with trimethoprim. His regular medications include metformin 500 mg BD, gliclazide MR 60 mg and lisinopril 10 mg. Staff report he has been refusing to drink for the last two days. On arrival he is drowsy, severely dehydrated and has a temperature of 38.4 degrees. Capillary glucose is 48 mmol/L. Venous gas shows pH 7.34, bicarbonate 20 mmol/L, anion gap 14, lactate 1.8. Capillary ketones are 0.6 mmol/L. Sodium 152 mmol/L, potassium 4.1, urea 24, creatinine 198 micromol/L (baseline 110), corrected sodium for glucose 158 mmol/L. Calculated effective serum osmolality is 348 mosm/kg. Urinalysis confirms leucocytes and nitrites; he is on no SGLT2 inhibitor. There are no Kussmaul respirations and no ketotic breath.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/56 mmHg, HR 112, RR 18, T 38.4, SpO2 96 percent on RA, GCS 13.
data_table.Examination: Severely dehydrated, dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 4 seconds. No Kussmaul breathing.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 48 mmol/L; ketones 0.6 mmol/L; pH 7.34, HCO3 20, AG 14; Na 152, K 4.1, Cr 198 (baseline 110); effective osmolality 348 mosm/kg.

lead_in: Which feature best distinguishes this presentation as hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS) rather than DKA?

options.text: The blood glucose level of 48 mmol/L on bedside capillary testing today
options.rationale: Severe hyperglycaemia occurs in both HHS and DKA; glucose level alone does not distinguish them (eTG Endocrinology - HHS).

options.text: Effective osmolality above 320, capillary ketones under 3 and pH above 7.30 without acidosis
options.rationale: Diagnostic features of HHS are marked hyperglycaemia (typically >30 mmol/L), effective osmolality >320 mosm/kg, minimal ketosis (capillary ketones <3 mmol/L) and absence of significant acidosis (pH >=7.30, HCO3 >=15) (JBDS HHS 2022; eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: The acute kidney injury with creatinine rising from baseline 110 to 198 micromol/L
options.rationale: AKI is common in both DKA and HHS from volume depletion and does not differentiate them (KDIGO; eTG).

options.text: The presence of a urinary tract infection acting as the precipitating event here
options.rationale: Infection precipitates both HHS and DKA; the precipitant is not the discriminator (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: The reduced Glasgow Coma Scale score on arrival to the emergency department
options.rationale: Drowsiness can occur in both, although depressed consciousness correlates more closely with osmolality. The defining feature remains the biochemistry pattern, not GCS alone (JBDS HHS 2022).

explanation.summary: HHS is defined biochemically by severe hyperglycaemia, effective osmolality >320 mosm/kg, minimal ketosis and absence of significant acidosis. Fluid resuscitation (typically 0.9 percent saline at a slower rate than DKA), VTE prophylaxis and identification of the precipitant are priorities; insulin is added at a lower rate (0.05 units/kg/hour) only after fluids restore perfusion (JBDS HHS 2022; eTG).
key_points: HHS biochemistry: glucose >30, osm >320, ketones <3, pH >=7.30, HCO3 >=15
Slower fluid replacement than DKA (older, comorbid, risk of fluid overload)
Mortality up to 20 percent - higher than DKA
Always give thromboprophylaxis (LMWH unless contraindicated)
explanation.pearls: If ketones are >3 mmol/L and there is acidosis, treat as mixed DKA/HHS using DKA pathway.

subtopic: Hypoglycaemia in T2DM on insulin - precipitant identification
stem: A 74-year-old woman with T2DM on metformin 1 g BD, gliclazide MR 120 mg daily and insulin glargine 22 units nocte is brought to ED by paramedics after her daughter found her diaphoretic and confused. Capillary glucose was 1.8 mmol/L pre-hospital. She received IM glucagon 1 mg followed by oral juice with rapid clinical improvement; her glucose now is 6.4 mmol/L and she is fully oriented. Over the last week she has reported anorexia, fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 2 kg. She started cefalexin five days ago for a urinary tract infection and the same day her GP added trimethoprim 300 mg daily after sensitivities returned. She has CKD stage 3b (eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2 baseline). She lives alone and reports occasional missed meals. Her last HbA1c three months ago was 49 mmol/mol (6.6 percent). She has no chest pain. ECG shows sinus rhythm rate 78 with no acute ischaemic changes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/72 mmHg, HR 78, T 36.6, SpO2 98 percent, GCS 15.
data_table.Examination: Now alert and oriented. No focal neurology. Mild dry mucous membranes.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose now 6.4 mmol/L; eGFR 38; HbA1c 49 mmol/mol; UEC otherwise normal.

lead_in: Which factor is most likely to have precipitated this hypoglycaemic episode?

options.text: The basal insulin glargine dose, which should be reduced as the default first action
options.rationale: Insulin glargine alone is unlikely to cause this severity of hypoglycaemia in a patient who has been stable on it. Identification of new precipitants takes priority over reflexive dose reduction (RACGP 2024; AMH).

options.text: Trimethoprim potentiating gliclazide via reduced clearance in CKD plus the reduced oral intake
options.rationale: Trimethoprim potentiates sulfonylureas (CYP2C9 inhibition and competitive renal tubular secretion) and gliclazide accumulates with reduced eGFR; together with poor intake and weight loss, this is the most likely precipitant. Sulfonylureas should be reviewed in CKD and with new interacting antibiotics (AMH; eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Direct metformin-induced hypoglycaemia from her usual twice-daily 1 g metformin dose
options.rationale: Metformin does not cause hypoglycaemia in monotherapy and only rarely contributes in combination; it is not the driver here (AMH).

options.text: Cefalexin-induced hypoglycaemia from the recently started urinary tract infection antibiotic
options.rationale: Cefalexin is not a recognised cause of hypoglycaemia; among antibiotics, fluoroquinolones and trimethoprim are the main offenders with sulfonylureas (AMH).

options.text: Inadequate paramedic glucagon dose given prior to her arrival in the emergency department
options.rationale: 1 mg IM glucagon is the standard adult dose and her glucose responded; this is not the cause of the original event (ANZCOR; AMH).

explanation.summary: Severe hypoglycaemia in an older patient on a sulfonylurea plus basal insulin should prompt review of recently introduced interacting drugs (trimethoprim, fluoroquinolones, fluconazole) and declining renal function. Sulfonylureas are best avoided in significant CKD and should usually be ceased before reflexively reducing insulin (RACGP 2024; AMH).
key_points: Trimethoprim, fluoroquinolones, fluconazole potentiate sulfonylureas
Sulfonylureas accumulate in CKD - avoid below eGFR ~30 to 45 mL/min/1.73m^2
Always look for missed meals, weight loss, new infections, new drugs
Older adults with HbA1c <53 mmol/mol on sulfonylurea + insulin are over-treated
explanation.pearls: Hypoglycaemia unawareness develops with recurrent lows - target a slightly higher HbA1c in frail older adults (53 to 64 mmol/mol).

subtopic: Thyroid storm vs myxoedema coma - recognition
stem: A 38-year-old woman is brought to ED with two days of worsening agitation, vomiting and fever after stopping carbimazole 10 days ago because she ran out of her script. She was diagnosed with Graves disease six months ago. On arrival she is agitated, sweaty and tremulous. She is in atrial fibrillation at 152 bpm with a blood pressure of 96/52 mmHg. Temperature is 39.6 degrees and she has had one episode of vomiting in ED. She has bilateral exophthalmos and a smooth diffuse goitre. Her last TSH four weeks ago was <0.01 mIU/L with free T4 48 pmol/L. She is alert but confused (GCS 14) and not following commands consistently. There is no chest pain. Bedside echo at the bedside shows preserved LV function. Capillary glucose is 7.2 mmol/L. She has no recent travel, no respiratory symptoms and clear chest on auscultation. Beta-hCG is negative. CRP is 18 mg/L and white cell count is 11.2 x10^9/L without left shift. She has no recent travel, no known infectious contacts and her urinalysis is negative. There is no rash and no neck stiffness. The endocrinology registrar has been called. She has no documented allergy to iodine, propranolol, propylthiouracil, carbimazole or hydrocortisone.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/52 mmHg, HR 152 (AF), RR 28, T 39.6 degC, SpO2 97 percent, GCS 14.
data_table.Examination: Agitated, tremulous, diaphoretic. Lid lag, exophthalmos, smooth goitre with bruit. Warm peripheries.
data_table.Investigations: TSH <0.01, fT4 48 (4 weeks ago); ECG AF rate 152; CXR clear; CRP 18; WCC 11.2; LFTs mildly deranged ALT 78.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial medical management?

options.text: IV propranolol, oral propylthiouracil, Lugol's iodine after PTU, hydrocortisone, paracetamol and active cooling
options.rationale: Thyroid storm (Burch-Wartofsky >=45) requires beta blockade, an antithyroid drug (PTU preferred for additional T4-to-T3 conversion blockade), iodine started AFTER the antithyroid drug to prevent substrate loading, hydrocortisone (blocks conversion and treats relative adrenal insufficiency) and supportive care including cooling and treatment of the precipitant (eTG Endocrinology; AMH).

options.text: IV amiodarone 300 mg for prompt rate control of her new atrial fibrillation
options.rationale: Amiodarone is high in iodine and worsens thyroid storm; beta blockade is preferred for rate control. Amiodarone is contraindicated (AMH).

options.text: Empirical IV ceftriaxone 2 g and acyclovir 10 mg/kg for presumed sepsis or encephalitis
options.rationale: The clinical picture fits thyroid storm with a clear precipitant (medication non-adherence); empirical antimicrobials may be reasonable if infection is suspected but they do not address the thyroid emergency (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: IV levothyroxine 200 microgram and IV hydrocortisone 100 mg for presumed myxoedema coma
options.rationale: Myxoedema coma presents with hypothermia, bradycardia, hypoventilation and depressed consciousness; this patient has hyperthermia, tachyarrhythmia and tremor - the opposite picture (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Oral carbimazole 60 mg single dose only and immediate discharge home with outpatient endocrinology follow-up
options.rationale: Thyroid storm has a mortality of 10 to 30 percent without aggressive inpatient management; discharge is unsafe regardless of antithyroid drug given (eTG Endocrinology).

explanation.summary: Thyroid storm is a clinical diagnosis (Burch-Wartofsky score) with hyperthermia, tachyarrhythmia, CNS dysfunction, GI symptoms and a precipitant on a background of hyperthyroidism. Management combines beta blockade, antithyroid drug (PTU or carbimazole), iodine (after antithyroid drug), corticosteroid and treatment of the precipitant. Myxoedema coma is the opposite picture and requires IV thyroid hormone and hydrocortisone (eTG; AMH).
key_points: PTU preferred in storm (blocks peripheral T4->T3 conversion)
Iodine (Lugol's or potassium iodide) given >=1 hour AFTER antithyroid drug
Hydrocortisone covers relative adrenal insufficiency and blocks T4->T3
Avoid amiodarone; avoid aspirin (displaces T4 from binding proteins)
explanation.pearls: Myxoedema coma classically: hypothermia, bradycardia, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia, hypoventilation, depressed GCS - mirror image of storm.

subtopic: Graves disease - definitive treatment choice
stem: A 34-year-old woman is referred by her GP for definitive management of Graves disease diagnosed 14 months ago. She was treated with carbimazole 20 mg daily for 12 months, achieving biochemical euthyroidism, but TSH-receptor antibody titres remain markedly elevated (TRAb 8 IU/L) and she relapsed within six weeks of stopping carbimazole. She has moderate active Graves orbitopathy with proptosis, mild diplopia on upgaze and intermittent retro-orbital pain; CAS (Clinical Activity Score) is 4/7. She has not yet completed her family and would like to conceive within the next 12 months. She is a non-smoker now (ex-smoker, ceased six months ago). Her thyroid is moderately enlarged without nodularity, and ultrasound shows no discrete nodules. She has no compressive symptoms. She has no contraindication to glucocorticoid prophylaxis. She lives with a partner and two pre-school children. She has no documented allergy to carbimazole or propylthiouracil, no prior neck surgery and no compressive symptoms. Her thyroid function tests are biochemically euthyroid on her current dose and her last full blood count and liver enzymes are normal. She has discussed all three options (long-term antithyroid drug, radioactive iodine and total thyroidectomy) with her endocrinologist. There is access to a high-volume thyroid surgeon at her local tertiary referral hospital, and pre-operative steroid prophylaxis is available if needed.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 88, weight 62 kg.
data_table.Examination: Diffuse smooth goitre, no nodules, no compressive symptoms. Proptosis with diplopia on upgaze, CAS 4/7.
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.2 mIU/L, fT4 14 pmol/L on carbimazole 20 mg; TRAb 8 IU/L (relapsed off treatment); thyroid US no nodules.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate definitive treatment option?

options.text: Radioactive iodine 131I ablation arranged within the next month as definitive therapy
options.rationale: RAI can worsen active Graves orbitopathy, especially with moderate-to-severe disease, smoking history and high TRAb; pregnancy must be avoided for 6 months after RAI, conflicting with her conception plans (ESA / ETA 2021).

options.text: Total thyroidectomy after pre-operative carbimazole and Lugol's iodine plus calcium and vitamin D loading
options.rationale: In Graves disease with moderate-to-severe active orbitopathy, large goitre, plans for pregnancy within 12 months, or patient preference, total thyroidectomy is preferred. It removes the antigenic stimulus, does not worsen orbitopathy and allows immediate trial of conception once levothyroxine is stable. Pre-operative preparation and a high-volume surgeon reduce complications (ETA 2021; eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Indefinite low-dose carbimazole 5 mg once daily as long-term outpatient monotherapy
options.rationale: Long-term low-dose carbimazole is an emerging option for selected patients but is less effective at definitive cure; high TRAb, relapse history and active orbitopathy favour definitive treatment, and carbimazole carries teratogenicity risk in early pregnancy (eTG; AMH).

options.text: Switch to propylthiouracil 100 mg three times daily continued indefinitely for ongoing control
options.rationale: PTU has a higher risk of severe hepatotoxicity than carbimazole and is reserved for first trimester of pregnancy or thyroid storm; it is not preferred for long-term outpatient management (AMH).

options.text: High-dose IV methylprednisolone 500 mg weekly for 6 weeks given alone as definitive treatment
options.rationale: IV methylprednisolone treats active moderate-to-severe orbitopathy but does not treat the underlying hyperthyroidism; it complements but does not replace definitive thyroid management (ETA 2021).

explanation.summary: Choice between carbimazole, radioactive iodine and thyroidectomy in Graves disease is guided by relapse risk (TRAb titre, goitre size, age, gender), pregnancy plans and orbitopathy status. Active moderate-to-severe orbitopathy is a relative contraindication to RAI; pregnancy within 6 months precludes RAI; large goitre or compressive symptoms favour surgery (ETA 2021; eTG).
key_points: Carbimazole 12 to 18 months: ~50 percent relapse after stopping
RAI: definitive but worsens orbitopathy in ~15 percent unless steroid prophylaxis given; defer pregnancy 6 months
Surgery: rapid cure, preserves option of pregnancy soon, removes antigen in severe orbitopathy
PTU reserved for first trimester pregnancy or thyroid storm
explanation.pearls: Smoking strongly worsens Graves orbitopathy and RAI outcomes - cessation is mandatory.

subtopic: Subclinical hypothyroidism (Hashimoto) - treatment threshold
stem: A 48-year-old woman attends her GP after blood tests ordered for tiredness 8 weeks ago showed TSH 7.2 mIU/L with normal free T4. A repeat panel today shows TSH 6.8 mIU/L, free T4 14 pmol/L (normal), and TPO antibodies markedly elevated at 540 IU/mL. She has no thyroid pain, no neck swelling and no family history of thyroid cancer. She has hypercholesterolaemia treated with atorvastatin and is otherwise well. She is not pregnant and is not planning pregnancy. Symptoms of fatigue have improved spontaneously over the last month. She has no constipation, no weight change beyond 1 kg and no menstrual irregularity. She has no goitre on examination, and there are no compressive symptoms. Her cardiovascular risk assessment is moderate (5-year CVD risk 7 percent). She is not on amiodarone or lithium. She does not smoke.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/76, HR 68, weight 72 kg.
data_table.Examination: No goitre. No dry skin. Reflexes normal.
data_table.Investigations: TSH 6.8 mIU/L (was 7.2); fT4 14 pmol/L; TPO Ab 540 IU/mL (positive); lipids stable.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence levothyroxine 50 microgram daily and review TFTs in 6 to 8 weeks
options.rationale: Treatment of subclinical hypothyroidism is recommended when TSH is persistently >=10 mIU/L, or 4 to 10 mIU/L with significant symptoms attributable to hypothyroidism, pregnancy planning, infertility, goitre or strong patient preference. None of these apply (ESA position; RACGP).

options.text: Repeat TFTs and TPO antibodies in 6 to 12 months, with no treatment now
options.rationale: With TSH between 4 and 10 mIU/L, minimal symptoms, no goitre and no pregnancy plans, observation with annual TFT monitoring is recommended in Hashimoto thyroiditis; ~25 to 50 percent progress to overt hypothyroidism (ESA position; RACGP Red Book).

options.text: Refer for ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration of the thyroid for cytology
options.rationale: FNA is indicated for suspicious thyroid nodules; there is no nodule on examination or imaging here (RACGP).

options.text: Commence triiodothyronine (T3, liothyronine) 20 microgram daily as primary thyroid replacement
options.rationale: T3 monotherapy is not recommended for primary hypothyroidism; levothyroxine T4 is the standard if treatment is initiated (AMH; ESA).

options.text: Order thyroid ultrasound today and add TSH receptor antibodies to her bloods
options.rationale: TSH receptor antibodies are useful in suspected Graves disease, not Hashimoto; ultrasound is reserved for palpable nodules or compressive symptoms (RACGP).

explanation.summary: Subclinical hypothyroidism is defined by an elevated TSH with a normal free T4. Treatment is generally reserved for TSH persistently >=10 mIU/L, or 4 to 10 with attributable symptoms, pregnancy, infertility or large goitre. Otherwise, monitoring is appropriate; positive TPO antibodies confirm Hashimoto and predict progression (ESA; RACGP).
key_points: Confirm with a repeat after 6 to 12 weeks before labelling
Treat if TSH >=10, symptomatic with TSH 4 to 10, pregnant or planning pregnancy
Start levothyroxine 1.6 microgram/kg/day (lower in elderly and IHD)
Recheck TFTs 6 to 8 weeks after dose changes
explanation.pearls: In pregnancy, treat subclinical hypothyroidism if TSH is above the trimester-specific upper limit (typically >4.0 mIU/L).

subtopic: Acute symptomatic hypocalcaemia post-thyroidectomy
stem: A 52-year-old woman is reviewed on the surgical ward 14 hours after total thyroidectomy for a multinodular goitre with compressive symptoms. She reports peri-oral and finger tingling for the last hour and one episode of carpal spasm when the cuff was inflated. She is otherwise well, with no airway compromise and no haematoma at the wound. She is on regular paracetamol and prophylactic enoxaparin. Bloods drawn at the onset of symptoms show: corrected calcium 1.78 mmol/L (corrected for albumin 38 g/L), ionised calcium 0.92 mmol/L, magnesium 0.74 mmol/L, phosphate 1.6 mmol/L, parathyroid hormone 0.4 pmol/L (low), 25-OH vitamin D 64 nmol/L, potassium 4.1, creatinine 76. Her ECG shows a prolonged QTc of 510 ms. She has no history of renal stones, no malignancy and her pre-operative calcium was 2.34 mmol/L. She is alert with GCS 15. There is no respiratory distress.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 84, RR 16, SpO2 98 percent on RA, T 36.7.
data_table.Examination: Peri-oral and finger paraesthesiae. Positive Chvostek and Trousseau signs. No stridor. Wound dry.
data_table.Investigations: Corrected Ca 1.78 mmol/L, ionised Ca 0.92; Mg 0.74; PTH 0.4 pmol/L; ECG QTc 510 ms.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Oral calcium carbonate 1.5 g elemental calcium with calcitriol 0.5 microgram and clinical review in 4 hours
options.rationale: Oral therapy is appropriate for asymptomatic mild hypocalcaemia or as maintenance after IV correction, but symptomatic acute hypocalcaemia with tetany or QT prolongation requires IV calcium (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: IV 10 percent calcium gluconate 10 mL bolus followed by infusion, replace magnesium, then oral calcium plus calcitriol
options.rationale: Symptomatic hypocalcaemia with tetany or QT prolongation requires IV 10 percent calcium gluconate bolus followed by infusion to prevent rebound. Magnesium replacement is required if low because it perpetuates hypocalcaemia. Oral calcium and active vitamin D (calcitriol) are added for maintenance, with active D needed because of low PTH (eTG Endocrinology; AMH). Practically: 10 mL of 10 percent calcium gluconate (2.2 mmol Ca) in 100 mL of 5 percent dextrose over 10 minutes, then infusion such as 100 mL of 10 percent calcium gluconate in 1 L over 6 to 8 hours, with active vitamin D needed because PTH is low.

options.text: IV 10 percent calcium chloride 10 mL given via a peripheral cannula stat dose
options.rationale: Calcium chloride contains three times more elemental calcium per mL but is highly irritant and risks severe extravasation injury via a peripheral line; calcium gluconate is preferred for peripheral administration (AMH; eTG).

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate 10 mmol over 20 minutes given as the only acute therapy
options.rationale: Magnesium 0.74 mmol/L is borderline and replacement is appropriate, but magnesium alone will not correct her acute symptomatic hypocalcaemia in this timeframe (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: IV cinacalcet 30 mg given as a single calcimimetic dose for acute symptom control
options.rationale: Cinacalcet is a calcimimetic that LOWERS calcium in hyperparathyroidism; it is contraindicated in hypocalcaemia (AMH).

explanation.summary: Symptomatic hypocalcaemia (tetany, seizures, laryngospasm, QT prolongation, prolonged ECG changes) is treated with IV 10 percent calcium gluconate bolus and infusion, replacement of magnesium if low, and transition to oral calcium plus active vitamin D (calcitriol or alfacalcidol) once stable. Calcitriol is required in hypoparathyroidism because PTH-dependent 1-alpha-hydroxylation is impaired (eTG; AMH).
key_points: 10 percent calcium gluconate 10 mL = 2.2 mmol elemental Ca (safer peripherally)
Always replete magnesium - low Mg causes refractory hypocalcaemia
Active vitamin D (calcitriol) needed when PTH is low
Continuous ECG monitoring during IV infusion
explanation.pearls: Post-thyroidectomy hypocalcaemia: check Ca at 6, 12 and 24 hours; transient post-op hypoparathyroidism is common, permanent in 1 to 3 percent.

subtopic: Hyperkalaemia with ECG changes - treatment ladder
stem: A 68-year-old man with diabetic nephropathy (eGFR 22 mL/min/1.73m^2) attends ED after his GP found hyperkalaemia on routine bloods. He takes perindopril 8 mg, spironolactone 25 mg (started two weeks ago for resistant hypertension) and metformin which was stopped on his GP's advice. He feels generally fatigued and has had mild palpitations but no syncope. He is haemodynamically stable. ED bloods confirm potassium 7.2 mmol/L (venous, repeat 7.0), creatinine 268 micromol/L (baseline 220), bicarbonate 18 mmol/L, glucose 8.4 mmol/L, calcium 2.31 mmol/L. ECG shows peaked T waves in V2 to V5, PR interval 220 ms and QRS 110 ms with normal axis; there are no ischaemic changes. He is on no other K-sparing medication and has not used potassium-containing salt substitutes. Urine output is preserved. There is no evidence of haemolysis on the sample. He has no salt substitute use at home, no recent NSAID use and no missed dialysis sessions. He has no pre-existing pacemaker.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 146/86, HR 72, RR 14, T 36.5, SpO2 97 percent.
data_table.Examination: Comfortable. No focal weakness. No pericardial rub.
data_table.Investigations: K+ 7.2 mmol/L; Cr 268 (baseline 220); HCO3 18; glucose 8.4; Ca 2.31; ECG peaked T waves V2-V5, PR 220, QRS 110.

lead_in: Which is the single most important immediate intervention?

options.text: IV 10 percent calcium gluconate 10 mL over 2 to 3 minutes to stabilise the myocardium
options.rationale: When hyperkalaemia is accompanied by ECG changes, the first action is calcium (gluconate 10 mL of 10 percent IV, or chloride if central access) to stabilise cardiac membranes; effect within minutes, duration 30 to 60 minutes. Repeat after 5 minutes if ECG not improved (eTG Endocrinology; ANZCOR ALS).

options.text: IV insulin actrapid 10 units with 50 mL of 50 percent dextrose to shift potassium
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose shifts potassium intracellularly and is part of the standard regimen, but it is given AFTER calcium when ECG changes are present; it does not stabilise the myocardium and takes 15 to 30 minutes for peak effect (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 10 to 20 mg via continuous oxygen-driven nebuliser at the bedside
options.rationale: Beta-2 agonists shift K+ intracellularly and complement insulin-dextrose, but again do not stabilise the myocardium and act in 15 to 30 minutes (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Oral resonium A (sodium polystyrene sulfonate) 30 g as the immediate priority intervention
options.rationale: Cation exchange resins remove K+ from the body but act over hours; they are useful for ongoing removal alongside dialysis planning but not as the immediate priority (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Urgent haemodialysis arranged after a CT scan to exclude obstructive uropathy first
options.rationale: Dialysis is definitive for refractory hyperkalaemia or fluid overload but takes time to arrange; emergency medical therapy must not be delayed for imaging or vascular access (eTG Endocrinology).

explanation.summary: Hyperkalaemia treatment ladder when ECG changes are present: (1) calcium gluconate (or chloride) IV to stabilise the myocardium, (2) insulin-dextrose AND nebulised salbutamol to shift K+ intracellularly, (3) cation exchange resin or potassium binder (patiromer, sodium zirconium cyclosilicate) for ongoing removal, (4) dialysis for refractory cases or end-organ failure (eTG Endocrinology; ANZCOR ALS).
key_points: Calcium first when ECG changes - stabilises within minutes
Insulin-dextrose: peaks at 30 to 60 minutes, monitor glucose for 6 hours
Stop precipitant drugs (ACEi/ARB, K-sparing diuretic, NSAID, trimethoprim)
Hold for 6 to 8 hours and recheck; arrange definitive removal
explanation.pearls: Peaked T waves first, then PR prolongation, then widened QRS, then sine-wave - call for help at any of the later stages.

subtopic: Hyponatraemia - SIADH vs cerebral salt wasting after SAH
stem: A 52-year-old man is on day 7 of his admission to neurosurgery after a Hunt-Hess 3 aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage clipped on day 1. On the morning ward round his nurse reports increased confusion and lethargy. His urine output over the last 24 hours has been 4.2 L. He has been on free water orally without IV fluids since day 4. Bloods today: sodium 122 mmol/L (was 134 on day 4), potassium 3.6, urea 9.4 mmol/L, creatinine 96 micromol/L, serum osmolality 258 mosm/kg, glucose 5.4 mmol/L. Urine: sodium 95 mmol/L, osmolality 540 mosm/kg, specific gravity 1.022. He is on nimodipine. Clinically he has dry mucous membranes, BP 102/64 mmHg (was 128/74 yesterday) lying with a 14 mmHg systolic drop on standing, HR 96, normal JVP and no oedema. Bedside lung ultrasound shows A-lines bilaterally. Weight is down 2 kg from admission. He has no haematuria, no glycosuria and no recent contrast. He is being managed on the neurosurgery ward under a neurosurgery-led delayed cerebral ischaemia protocol that targets euvolaemia and avoids hypotonic fluids. His transcranial Doppler velocities yesterday were not elevated and he has had no new focal neurological deficit. His preceding bloods on day 4 had been normal apart from mild hypokalaemia. He has had no diuretic, no mannitol, and no recent IV contrast load. Cortisol stimulation is normal, thyroid function is normal, and he has not received DDAVP. The team are considering whether the underlying mechanism is SIADH or cerebral salt wasting, because the management of these two conditions is opposite and the wrong choice risks delayed cerebral ischaemia, rebleeding or osmotic demyelination.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 102/64 lying with 14 mmHg systolic postural drop, HR 96, T 36.7, SpO2 98 percent.
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, JVP not visible, no oedema, weight down 2 kg.
data_table.Investigations: Na 122 mmol/L (was 134), K 3.6, urea 9.4, Cr 96, serum osm 258; urine Na 95, urine osm 540; UO 4.2 L/24h.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial fluid management?

options.text: Fluid restriction to 800 mL per day with daily sodium monitoring on the ward
options.rationale: Fluid restriction is appropriate for SIADH (euvolaemic), but this patient is hypovolaemic with polyuria, weight loss and postural hypotension after SAH - restricting fluid would worsen cerebral hypoperfusion and the risk of delayed cerebral ischaemia (Australasian Stroke Foundation Guidelines; ENLS 2023).

options.text: IV 0.9 percent sodium chloride 125 mL/hour with oral salt or 3 percent saline if sodium fails to rise
options.rationale: The clinical picture (polyuria, weight loss, postural hypotension, dry mucous membranes after SAH) plus high urine sodium fits cerebral salt wasting (CSW), where treatment is volume and sodium replacement, not restriction. Maintaining euvolaemia and avoiding hyponatraemia reduces delayed cerebral ischaemia in SAH (Australian Stroke Foundation; ENLS 2023).

options.text: IV tolvaptan 15 mg orally given today with hourly sodium monitoring on the ward
options.rationale: Tolvaptan (vasopressin V2 antagonist) treats SIADH by increasing free water clearance; it would worsen hypovolaemic hyponatraemia and is not appropriate here (AMH).

options.text: IV 3 percent hypertonic saline 150 mL bolus over 10 minutes only, with no further fluid
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline bolus is appropriate for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia with seizures or coma (target rise 4 to 6 mmol/L), but a single bolus without ongoing maintenance volume in CSW will under-treat the volume depletion and risk overshooting sodium correction (ENLS 2023).

options.text: IV 5 percent dextrose at 100 mL/hour to reduce osmotic stress on the brain
options.rationale: Free water in 5 percent dextrose worsens hyponatraemia and is contraindicated; in any neurocritical patient it also increases intracranial water content (ENLS 2023).

explanation.summary: After SAH or other brain injury, distinguishing SIADH from CSW depends on volume status. Both have low serum osmolality and inappropriately concentrated urine with high urine sodium. CSW shows hypovolaemia (weight loss, postural hypotension, dry mucous membranes, polyuria); SIADH shows euvolaemia. CSW is treated with isotonic saline plus sodium replacement; SIADH with fluid restriction (or tolvaptan if persistent). Limit sodium correction to 8 to 10 mmol/L per 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination (ENLS 2023; Australian Stroke Foundation).
key_points: Volume status is THE distinguishing feature between SIADH and CSW
After SAH, hypotonic fluids are avoided; isotonic or hypertonic preferred
Symptomatic severe hyponatraemia: 3 percent saline 150 mL bolus, target rise 4 to 6 mmol/L
Limit correction to 8 to 10 mmol/L per 24 h (6 to 8 if high risk for ODS)
explanation.pearls: If sodium overcorrects, re-lower with DDAVP and 5 percent dextrose - cerebral pontine myelinolysis is devastating and preventable.

subtopic: Acute ischaemic stroke - thrombolysis and thrombectomy eligibility
stem: A 68-year-old right-handed man is brought to a metropolitan ED by ambulance. He was last seen well at 0815 by his wife; she returned at 1005 to find him slumped on the kitchen floor with right-sided weakness and aphasia. Time of arrival is 1110 (2 hours 55 minutes after last seen well). He has hypertension, ischaemic heart disease, atrial fibrillation (CHA2DS2-VA 4) on apixaban 5 mg BD with his last dose at 0700. He has no history of recent surgery, head injury, bleeding or stroke. On arrival his blood pressure is 198/112 mmHg, glucose 6.8 mmol/L, GCS 12 (E3 V3 M6) and NIHSS is 18 (gaze preference, right hemiplegia, global aphasia, left neglect). Non-contrast CT brain shows no haemorrhage and an ASPECTS of 8 in the left MCA territory. CT angiography demonstrates a proximal left M1 occlusion with reasonable collaterals. There is a comprehensive stroke centre with thrombectomy capability 15 minutes away by road. There is no recent gastrointestinal bleeding, no recent intracranial surgery, no platelet count below 100 and no documented INR above 1.7. He has consented for thrombectomy through his next of kin and the neurointerventional team have been activated. He has no known iodinated contrast allergy.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 198/112, HR 92 (AF), T 36.8, SpO2 97 percent on RA, GCS 12.
data_table.Examination: Right hemiplegia, global aphasia, left gaze preference. NIHSS 18.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 6.8 mmol/L; INR not on warfarin; apixaban 5 mg BD with last dose 0700; CT brain no haemorrhage, ASPECTS 8; CTA proximal M1 occlusion.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step in acute reperfusion management?

options.text: Administer IV alteplase immediately and then transfer for mechanical thrombectomy at the comprehensive stroke centre
options.rationale: IV thrombolysis is contraindicated within 48 hours of a direct oral anticoagulant unless DOAC levels are confirmed sub-therapeutic; his last apixaban dose was at 0700. Transfer for mechanical thrombectomy is preferred without thrombolysis (Australian Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines).

options.text: Lower BP to under 140/90 mmHg with IV labetalol before making any reperfusion decision
options.rationale: BP target prior to thrombolysis is <185/110 mmHg; before thrombectomy without thrombolysis higher BP is tolerated. Aggressive lowering risks penumbral hypoperfusion (Australian Stroke Foundation; ENLS).

options.text: Activate stroke team and arrange urgent transfer for thrombectomy without thrombolysis, BP <185/110
options.rationale: Within 6 hours of last seen well, with a proximal M1 occlusion, NIHSS >=6 and ASPECTS >=6, mechanical thrombectomy is indicated. IV thrombolysis is contraindicated due to recent apixaban; proceed directly to thrombectomy. BP target pre-procedure is <185/110 (Australian Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines).

options.text: Administer aspirin 300 mg orally and admit to the stroke unit for conservative management
options.rationale: Antiplatelet within 24 hours of thrombolysis is contraindicated and antiplatelet alone is not the reperfusion treatment for a proximal LVO; thrombectomy is the standard of care (Australian Stroke Foundation).

options.text: Administer IV idarucizumab to reverse apixaban first, then proceed to IV thrombolysis with alteplase
options.rationale: Idarucizumab reverses dabigatran, not apixaban; andexanet alfa partially reverses factor Xa inhibitors but is not generally used to enable thrombolysis. Direct transfer to thrombectomy is preferred (AMH; Australian Stroke Foundation).

explanation.summary: Acute ischaemic stroke reperfusion choices: IV thrombolysis within 4.5 hours of last seen well if no contraindication (BP <185/110, no recent DOAC dose within 48 h unless levels confirmed sub-therapeutic, no recent major surgery/bleeding/stroke). Mechanical thrombectomy within 6 hours for anterior circulation LVO (M1/ICA) with ASPECTS >=6 and disabling deficit; window extends to 24 hours if perfusion imaging shows salvageable penumbra. Thrombectomy is delivered with or without prior thrombolysis (Australian Stroke Foundation).
key_points: DOAC within 48 hours is a relative contraindication to thrombolysis unless levels measured
Thrombectomy window 0 to 6 h with CT/CTA; 6 to 24 h with perfusion imaging (DEFUSE-3, DAWN criteria)
Pre-thrombolysis BP target <185/110 mmHg
DAPT (aspirin + clopidogrel) for 21 days then aspirin alone after minor stroke / high-risk TIA (CHANCE / POINT)
explanation.pearls: Door-to-needle target 60 minutes; door-to-groin target 90 minutes - process matters more than algorithm minutiae.

subtopic: TIA - ABCD2 risk stratification and disposition
stem: A 72-year-old woman attends her GP three hours after an episode of left arm and face weakness lasting 35 minutes that has now completely resolved. She has hypertension treated with amlodipine, type 2 diabetes on metformin and a long-standing 20 pack-year smoking history (current smoker). She has no atrial fibrillation on a previous Holter. Examination is now normal. Her BP today is 168/96 mmHg, blood glucose 9.2 mmol/L, ECG shows sinus rhythm. She has had no prior stroke or TIA. There is no carotid bruit. She has no recent head injury, no anticoagulation and no known bleeding tendency. She has no chest pain or palpitations. Her ABCD2 score is calculated. The GP is considering whether to manage her in primary care with same-week clinic follow-up or to refer urgently to the ED stroke pathway today.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/96, HR 78, weight 76 kg.
data_table.Examination: Normal neurological examination now. No carotid bruit. ECG sinus rhythm.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 9.2 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate disposition?

options.text: Same-day urgent ED referral for brain and vessel imaging, aspirin 300 mg loading and stroke pathway admission
options.rationale: Current Australian Stroke Foundation guidance recommends urgent (same-day) assessment for all patients with suspected TIA, regardless of ABCD2 score, due to high early stroke risk especially in the first 48 hours. Imaging of brain and vessels and immediate antiplatelet (or DAPT for 21 days after minor TIA / stroke) reduces recurrence (Australian Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines).

options.text: Reassure and arrange outpatient TIA clinic review in 2 weeks with aspirin 100 mg daily started today
options.rationale: Two-week deferral risks recurrent stroke; modern Australian guidance recommends same-day specialist assessment regardless of ABCD2 (Australian Stroke Foundation).

options.text: Arrange a carotid Doppler within the next one month and review her again after that
options.rationale: Carotid imaging is appropriate but delays reperfusion-relevant decisions; urgent imaging same day is the standard (Australian Stroke Foundation).

options.text: Commence clopidogrel 75 mg daily and refer for cardiology rhythm monitoring only without imaging
options.rationale: While rhythm monitoring is important, clopidogrel alone in primary care without urgent imaging misses the time-critical window for recurrent stroke prevention (Australian Stroke Foundation).

options.text: Calculate ABCD2 first; if 3 or less manage in primary care, if 4 or more refer urgently
options.rationale: ABCD2 is no longer used in current Australian guidance to decide disposition; all suspected TIAs warrant urgent (same-day) specialist assessment because lower scores still carry meaningful early stroke risk (Australian Stroke Foundation).

explanation.summary: Modern Australian guidance for TIA emphasises urgent same-day specialist assessment regardless of ABCD2 score. Brain and vascular imaging, antiplatelet initiation (DAPT for 21 days after high-risk TIA or minor non-cardioembolic stroke per CHANCE/POINT), risk factor control and rhythm monitoring for occult AF underpin secondary prevention (Australian Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines).
key_points: All suspected TIAs: same-day specialist assessment
Aspirin 300 mg loading, then DAPT 21 days for high-risk TIA / minor stroke
BP, lipid, glucose, smoking cessation, anticoagulation if AF detected
Carotid imaging within 24 to 48 hours for anterior circulation events
explanation.pearls: ABCD2 is still useful as a teaching tool but does not safely triage TIA to deferred care.

subtopic: First seizure in adult - workup and Australian driving advice
stem: A 34-year-old man is brought to ED by his partner after a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 90 seconds while watching television. He has no prior seizures, no head injury, no drug or alcohol use and takes no medications. He was confused for about 20 minutes after the event and bit the side of his tongue. On arrival he is back to his baseline. Capillary glucose, electrolytes, calcium, magnesium and a urine drug screen are normal. ECG is normal sinus rhythm. He works as a self-employed delivery driver using a standard private licence (no commercial endorsement). His non-contrast CT brain is normal. He wants to know when he can drive and what investigations are needed. He has no fever, no headache, no neck stiffness and no focal neurology. He has no family history of epilepsy and no recent travel. He is otherwise well and has not started any new medications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78, HR 76, T 36.8, SpO2 99 percent, GCS 15.
data_table.Examination: Normal neurology. Lateral tongue bite. No focal weakness.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose normal; electrolytes, Ca, Mg, drug screen all normal; CT brain normal; ECG normal.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate combination of next investigation and driving advice?

options.text: MRI brain and EEG as outpatient; no driving for at least 6 months (Austroads private standard)
options.rationale: First unprovoked seizure workup includes outpatient MRI brain (preferred over CT for structural lesions) and EEG to assess recurrence risk. Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive 2022 requires a minimum 6-month non-driving period after a first seizure for private licence, longer for commercial. The treating doctor must counsel and document this (Austroads AFTD 2022; eTG Neurology).

options.text: Commence sodium valproate immediately and clear him to return to driving in 1 month
options.rationale: Antiepileptic medication after a single unprovoked seizure with no abnormalities is not routinely recommended; reduction in recurrence is offset by drug burden. 1-month driving suspension is insufficient under Australian standards and valproate is teratogenic (eTG Neurology; Austroads).

options.text: Lumbar puncture today and start IV ceftriaxone for presumed bacterial meningitis empirically
options.rationale: There are no features of meningitis (no fever, headache, neck stiffness, altered consciousness); LP is not indicated (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Continuous video-EEG admission for 72 hours starting today as the immediate next investigation
options.rationale: Continuous video-EEG is for diagnostic uncertainty (PNES vs epilepsy) or pre-surgical evaluation, not standard first-seizure workup (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Cleared to return to commercial driving as soon as he feels well, neurology in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Commercial driving requires longer abstinence (typically 5 years seizure-free off medication or 10 years with conditions) under Austroads; immediate return is unsafe and legally non-compliant (Austroads AFTD 2022).

explanation.summary: After a first unprovoked seizure in an adult, MRI brain and EEG outpatient form the standard recurrence-risk workup. Antiepileptic drug initiation is not routine but may be considered if recurrence risk is high (epileptiform EEG, structural lesion). Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive 2022 mandates a minimum 6-month non-driving period for private vehicles after a first seizure (longer with risk factors), and longer abstinence for commercial vehicles (eTG Neurology).
key_points: Workup: MRI brain, EEG, electrolytes, glucose, calcium, magnesium, drug screen
AED start considered if recurrence risk high (e.g. structural lesion, epileptiform EEG, nocturnal seizure)
Private vehicle: minimum 6 months off driving after first seizure
Commercial vehicle: minimum 5 years seizure-free off medication
explanation.pearls: Counselling and documentation of driving restriction is the doctor's legal and professional duty - patients self-report to road authority.

subtopic: Status epilepticus - second-line management
stem: A 56-year-old man with known epilepsy on sodium valproate is brought to ED in convulsive status epilepticus. The seizure started 25 minutes ago in the ambulance. Paramedics gave 10 mg IM midazolam at 12 minutes and another 10 mg IV at 20 minutes without resolution; he continues to have generalised tonic-clonic activity. On arrival his airway is maintained with nasopharyngeal airway and SpO2 96 percent on 15 L non-rebreather. BP is 156/92, HR 124, temperature 37.1. Capillary glucose is 7.4 mmol/L. He has IV access and is now 27 minutes into the seizure. He has no recent head injury and his valproate level is pending. There is no fever, no rash, and no obvious focal sepsis. His airway position is maintained and there is no audible stridor. His pupils are equal and reactive. Bedside ECG shows sinus tachycardia without ischaemic changes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 156/92, HR 124, RR 18 (assisted), T 37.1, SpO2 96 percent on 15 L NRB, GCS 3 during seizure.
data_table.Examination: Generalised tonic-clonic seizure continuing. Airway maintained with NPA.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 7.4 mmol/L; valproate level pending; ECG sinus tachycardia.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat a further IV midazolam 10 mg bolus dose as third benzodiazepine attempt
options.rationale: He has already received two adequate benzodiazepine doses with no response; further benzodiazepine risks respiratory depression without added benefit. Escalate to a second-line agent (eTG Neurology; ANZCOR).

options.text: IV levetiracetam 60 mg/kg (maximum 4.5 g) given over 10 minutes as second-line agent
options.rationale: After two adequate doses of a benzodiazepine, second-line therapy in convulsive status epilepticus is IV levetiracetam, phenytoin (or fos-phenytoin), or sodium valproate. Levetiracetam at 60 mg/kg over 10 minutes is recommended by ESETT and current Australian eTG and NSW Health guidance (eTG Neurology; ESETT 2019).

options.text: IV propofol 2 mg/kg induction and intubate without any further antiepileptic drug given
options.rationale: Anaesthetic induction is third-line, after a second-line antiepileptic has failed; jumping to anaesthesia first risks underestimating refractory disease and committing to ICU intubation unnecessarily (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Oral phenytoin 300 mg administered via nasogastric tube as the next antiepileptic agent
options.rationale: Oral phenytoin via NG tube has unpredictable absorption and is too slow for acute status; IV phenytoin (20 mg/kg, max rate 50 mg/min) is the parenteral option (eTG Neurology; AMH).

options.text: IV thiamine 200 mg and 50 mL of 50 percent dextrose given as the next intervention
options.rationale: Empirical glucose is reserved for hypoglycaemia; his glucose is 7.4 mmol/L. Thiamine is appropriate if alcohol use disorder is suspected but does not terminate the seizure (eTG Neurology).

explanation.summary: Status epilepticus management: first-line is benzodiazepine (IM midazolam 10 mg or IV lorazepam 4 mg, repeatable once); second-line after 20 minutes is IV levetiracetam 60 mg/kg, sodium valproate 40 mg/kg, or phenytoin 20 mg/kg (ESETT showed equivalence); third-line is induction with propofol, midazolam infusion or thiopental in ICU with intubation. Continuous EEG once anaesthetised (eTG Neurology; ESETT 2019).
key_points: Two benzodiazepine doses then escalate, do not repeat indefinitely
Second-line: levetiracetam, phenytoin or valproate (ESETT showed equivalent efficacy)
Refractory (>30 min despite second-line): intubation and continuous anaesthetic infusion
Always check glucose; consider thiamine, eclampsia, hyponatraemia, toxic causes
explanation.pearls: Do not delay treatment for imaging in convulsive status - terminate the seizure first, image when safe.

subtopic: Giant cell arteritis - recognition and management
stem: A 74-year-old woman presents to her GP with a three-week history of new bilateral temporal headache that has progressively worsened. She describes scalp tenderness when brushing her hair and jaw pain when chewing tough food that resolves with rest. She has had transient blurring of vision in her right eye for about 5 minutes yesterday that has now resolved. She has no rash, no fever and no neck stiffness. She has hypertension and osteoarthritis. She has lost 2 kg over the last month with night sweats. On examination her temporal arteries are tender and pulsatile bilaterally. There is no scalp ulceration. Her visual acuity is 6/9 right and 6/6 left with normal fundoscopy today and intact visual fields. Bloods: ESR 92 mm/hr, CRP 84 mg/L, Hb 108 g/L (normochromic normocytic), platelets 484 x10^9/L. There is no family history of vasculitis or other rheumatological disease. She has had no recent trauma.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 142/82, HR 72, T 36.9, SpO2 98 percent.
data_table.Examination: Tender pulsatile bilateral temporal arteries. Normal fundoscopy. VA 6/9 R, 6/6 L.
data_table.Investigations: ESR 92, CRP 84, Hb 108 (NCNC), platelets 484.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Reassure, prescribe simple analgesia and arrange neurology review in 4 weeks time
options.rationale: Untreated GCA can cause permanent monocular blindness from anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy; delayed treatment is contraindicated. Transient visual disturbance is a red flag (eTG Rheumatology; ARA position).

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 60 mg today and arrange temporal artery biopsy within 1 to 2 weeks
options.rationale: Clinical suspicion of GCA mandates immediate high-dose corticosteroid before biopsy confirmation (40 to 60 mg prednisolone daily, or 1 g IV methylprednisolone for visual loss) to prevent blindness. Biopsy should occur within 1 to 2 weeks before steroid alters histology (eTG Rheumatology).

options.text: Order CT brain today and discharge her home pending the imaging results being reported
options.rationale: CT brain does not diagnose GCA and delaying corticosteroid risks visual loss within hours to days (eTG Rheumatology).

options.text: Treat empirically as a migraine attack with oral sumatriptan 50 mg given immediately
options.rationale: New severe headache in a person over 50 with raised inflammatory markers, jaw claudication and visual symptoms is not migraine; triptans are contraindicated in suspected ischaemic disease (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Commence azathioprine 2 mg/kg as the steroid-sparing first-line monotherapy from today onwards
options.rationale: Steroid-sparing agents (methotrexate, tocilizumab) are added later for relapse or steroid toxicity; they do not replace immediate high-dose corticosteroid (eTG Rheumatology).

explanation.summary: Giant cell arteritis is a medical emergency due to risk of permanent visual loss. Diagnosis is clinical (age >=50, new headache, jaw claudication, temporal artery tenderness, raised ESR/CRP) supported by temporal artery biopsy (skip lesions; sensitivity ~75 percent) or vascular ultrasound (halo sign). High-dose corticosteroid is started immediately, with biopsy within 1 to 2 weeks. Tocilizumab is steroid-sparing in established disease (eTG Rheumatology).
key_points: Visual symptoms or jaw claudication: IV methylprednisolone 0.5 to 1 g daily for 3 days then oral prednisolone
Otherwise: oral prednisolone 40 to 60 mg daily
Aspirin 100 mg daily reduces ischaemic complications (controversial; review individual bleeding risk)
Tocilizumab and methotrexate are steroid-sparing options
Bone protection (calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonate), PCP prophylaxis if high-dose long-duration steroid
explanation.pearls: ESR and CRP are usually both elevated; isolated normal CRP makes GCA very unlikely.

subtopic: Subarachnoid haemorrhage - investigation pathway
stem: A 48-year-old previously well woman presents to ED 3 hours after the abrupt onset of the worst headache of her life while gardening. The headache reached maximum intensity within seconds and was associated with one episode of vomiting. She has no neurological deficits now, GCS 15, no neck stiffness, no rash and no photophobia. BP is 162/94 mmHg. She is not anticoagulated and has no recent trauma. She has no significant past medical history and is on no medications. There is no family history of intracranial aneurysm or polycystic kidney disease. Her examination including cranial nerves, fundi, motor and sensory testing is normal. Capillary glucose is normal. ECG shows sinus rhythm. She has had similar but less severe headaches with exertion before, never investigated.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 162/94, HR 88, T 36.8, SpO2 99 percent, GCS 15.
data_table.Examination: Normal neurology including fundoscopy. No meningism.
data_table.Investigations: Capillary glucose normal; ECG sinus rhythm.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial investigation?

options.text: Non-contrast CT brain performed within 6 hours of headache onset
options.rationale: Non-contrast CT brain within 6 hours of headache onset, reported by an experienced radiologist using a modern (third generation) scanner, has sensitivity approaching 100 percent for subarachnoid haemorrhage and is the first-line investigation. If negative beyond 6 hours, lumbar puncture for xanthochromia (after 12 hours) is added (eTG Neurology; ACEM).

options.text: Lumbar puncture for xanthochromia performed as the first-line acute investigation
options.rationale: Xanthochromia takes at least 12 hours to develop after subarachnoid bleeding; LP first is invasive and lower yield. CT brain is the first investigation (eTG Neurology).

options.text: MRI brain with gadolinium performed as the first-line acute investigation
options.rationale: MRI is sensitive for subacute SAH (FLAIR) but is not the first-line acute test; access and time make CT first-line (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Empirical IV ceftriaxone 2 g and dexamethasone 10 mg for presumed bacterial meningitis
options.rationale: There are no features of meningitis (no fever, neck stiffness, photophobia). The presentation is classical thunderclap headache and requires imaging for SAH (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Discharge with simple analgesia and outpatient neurology review in 1 week
options.rationale: Thunderclap headache is a red flag for SAH and other secondary causes; discharge without imaging is unsafe (eTG Neurology).

explanation.summary: Thunderclap headache reaching maximum severity within seconds to a minute is investigated with non-contrast CT brain (within 6 hours, sensitivity ~100 percent). Beyond 6 hours, LP for xanthochromia (after 12 hours from onset) confirms or excludes SAH. Positive findings require CT angiography and neurosurgical consultation for aneurysm management (eTG Neurology).
key_points: Thunderclap headache definition: maximal severity within 60 seconds
CT sensitivity ~100 percent within 6 hours, drops to ~85 percent at 24 hours
LP for xanthochromia from 12 hours after onset
CT angiography after positive findings to plan aneurysm intervention
Other thunderclap causes: cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, RCVS, dissection, pituitary apoplexy
explanation.pearls: Nimodipine 60 mg orally every 4 hours reduces delayed cerebral ischaemia after confirmed aneurysmal SAH.

subtopic: Adult anaphylaxis - ED management
stem: A 28-year-old woman is brought to ED after eating a peanut-containing dessert at a restaurant 25 minutes ago. She has a known peanut allergy and forgot her EpiPen at home. On arrival she is stridulous and dyspnoeic with widespread urticaria over her trunk and limbs and lip swelling. BP is 78/42 mmHg, HR 132, SpO2 91 percent on 15 L non-rebreather, RR 28. She is alert but distressed. She has no chest pain. There is no past history of cardiac disease and she is on no regular medications. She has had two previous milder reactions to nuts treated with oral antihistamines. She has not eaten any new foods, taken any new medications or had any recent insect stings. She has IV access in the right antecubital fossa. She has no documented contraindication to intramuscular adrenaline.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 78/42, HR 132, RR 28, SpO2 91 percent on 15 L NRB, T 36.6, GCS 15.
data_table.Examination: Stridor, widespread urticaria, lip swelling, accessory muscle use.
data_table.Investigations: Bedside investigations pending.

lead_in: What is the single most important immediate intervention?

options.text: IV hydrocortisone 200 mg given as a single bolus dose now
options.rationale: Corticosteroids do not reverse acute anaphylaxis and have no proven benefit in preventing biphasic reactions; they are not first-line and must not delay adrenaline (ASCIA Guidelines 2024; ANZCOR).

options.text: IM adrenaline 1:1000 0.5 mg into the anterolateral mid-thigh, repeated every 5 minutes
options.rationale: IM adrenaline 1:1000 0.5 mg (10 microgram/kg, max 0.5 mg per dose) into the anterolateral mid-thigh is the first-line and life-saving treatment for anaphylaxis. Repeat every 5 minutes if no response; the doses are anchored to the ASCIA and ANZCOR algorithms (ASCIA 2024; ANZCOR).

options.text: IV promethazine 25 mg as a slow intravenous bolus dose now
options.rationale: IV promethazine can cause hypotension and is not recommended in acute anaphylaxis; antihistamines treat skin features only (ASCIA 2024; AMH).

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg via oxygen-driven nebuliser at the bedside
options.rationale: Salbutamol is an adjunct for persistent wheeze after adrenaline but does not treat the systemic mediator release or upper airway oedema; IM adrenaline is the priority (ASCIA 2024).

options.text: IV 0.9 percent saline 1 L bolus given alone without any adrenaline
options.rationale: IV fluid is essential adjunctive therapy for distributive shock but does not reverse the underlying mediator release; adrenaline must be given first or simultaneously (ASCIA 2024).

explanation.summary: IM adrenaline is the single most important treatment in anaphylaxis. Adult dose is 0.5 mg of 1:1000 (10 microgram/kg, maximum 0.5 mg) into the anterolateral mid-thigh, repeated every 5 minutes as needed. Place supine with legs elevated unless airway compromise. Give high-flow oxygen and IV fluid (20 mL/kg crystalloid). Corticosteroids and antihistamines are adjuncts only (ASCIA 2024; ANZCOR).
key_points: IM adrenaline first, IV adrenaline infusion if refractory after 2 to 3 doses
Supine with legs elevated, high-flow oxygen, IV crystalloid bolus
Observe for biphasic reaction for at least 4 hours (longer if severe)
Discharge with EpiPen (or two), ASCIA action plan, allergy referral
explanation.pearls: If the patient is on a beta blocker and refractory, glucagon 1 to 5 mg IV may help by bypassing the beta receptor.
